text:
Riverside Neurology Centre

Outpatient Clinic Date: 14 October 2025

Dr Patel
Riverside Neurology Centre
12 Quayside Road, London SE10 4AB

Dear Dr Patel,

Emily Carter, DOB: 17-08-1989, Hospital No: C672154  NHS No. 4728395612
78 Willow Lane, London, W9 3HT

Current medication:
• LevETIRAcetam 500 mg twice daily
• Folic acid 5 mg once daily

History and interval update:
Emily Carter attended today reporting intermittent events over the past few months. She describes an episode characterised by a sudden break in awareness at home, witnessed by her partner, with a Seizure followed by postictal confusion lasting ~5 minute(s). She could not recall the immediate period after the event and felt fatigued thereafter. There are no reliable precipitants identified. She denies tongue-biting or incontinence. The exact occurrence pattern is not clear from her account and we were unable to establish a reliable count of events over a given time period.

Examination today was unremarkable with normal cardiorespiratory and neurological bedside assessment. There are no focal deficits on examination. Bloods from her GP last month (FBC, U&Es, LFTs) were within reference ranges per the shared record.

Plan:
• Continue current medications unchanged.
• We discussed maintaining a detailed event diary (including timing, context, recovery features, and any witnesses) and bringing this to the next review.
• Safety counselling reinforced (sleep regularity, alcohol moderation, and general first aid advice as previously provided).
• She will arrange for her partner to provide a short written account of the witnessed event for the record.
• Awaiting community EEG appointment already requested by the GP; results to be shared via the usual pathway once available.

Follow-up:
• Review in neurology clinic in 6 months, sooner if concerns arise.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: The exact occurrence pattern is not clear from her account and we were unable to establish a reliable count of events over a given time period.
Seizure followed by postictal confusion lasting ~5 minute(s)

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter confirms that the patient has had seizure events recently but explicitly states that the occurrence pattern is unclear and a reliable count could not be established. There are no explicit numeric frequencies provided, nor enough detail to infer a rate over day/week/month/year. Therefore, per the rules, when seizures are mentioned but frequency cannot be determined, the correct label is 'unknown.'
